plainMeaning: Thus all the everlasting and the man-made images of the moon-like Jinas have now been praised — by the venerable Mahendra, who composed this hymn.
recitation: evaṁ sāsaya sāsaya, paḍimā thuṇiyā jiṇiṁda-caṁdāṇaṁ. sirimaṁ mahiṁda bhavahiṁda, caṁda muṇivaṁda thua-mahiā.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. evaṁ < Skt. evam, 'thus'; a summative particle gathering all that precedes.
wordByWord.notes: Opens the colophon by sealing the whole foregoing act of praise: 'thus (have they all been praised).'
First of the pair; the beginningless cosmic Jina-images enshrined in the heavens and world-mountains.

wordByWord.etymology: Here standing for Pkt. a-sāsaya < Skt. a-śāśvata, 'non-eternal'; the negative 'a-' is elided in recitation/sandhi.
wordByWord.notes: Read as the contrasting member 'sāsaya a-sāsaya' = eternal and non-eternal, i.e. the historically founded shrines (see Variant note).

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. paḍimā < Skt. pratimā, 'image, likeness'; nom./acc. plural here.
wordByWord.notes: The object of the verb: it is the Jina-images, eternal and non-eternal alike, that have been hymned.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. past participle thuṇiya < √thuṇ / Skt. √stu (stuta), 'to praise, extol'; agreeing with paḍimā.

wordByWord.gloss: of the moon-like Lords of Jinas
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. jinendra-candrānām: jina + indra ('lord of Jinas') + candra ('moon'); genitive plural -āṇaṁ < Skt. -ānām.
wordByWord.notes: A tatpuruṣa with a metaphor: the Jinendras are 'moons'. Genitive: the images 'of' these moon-like supreme Jinas.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. sirimaṁ < Skt. śrīmān / śrīmat, 'possessing śrī (glory, fortune)'.
Pkt. mahiṁda < Skt. Mahendra ('great Indra'); the proper name of the author Mahendra(siṁha)sūri.
wordByWord.notes: The signature: this is the colophon naming the composer.

wordByWord.etymology: Likely Skt. bhava + indra ('lord of/amid bhava, existence') or bhavya + indra ('chief among the worthy souls'), Pkt. -hiṁda < -indra.
wordByWord.notes: An honorific epithet of Mahendra; exact resolution uncertain (see notes).
Echoes 'jiṇiṁda-caṁda' of line 1: the author too is a 'moon' — for the community of munis.

wordByWord.gloss: to / by the host of munis
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. muni-vṛnda ('assembly of sages'); some read muni-vandya ('revered by munis'). Pkt. muṇi + vaṁda.
wordByWord.notes: Either '[a moon] for the host of munis' or '[one] revered by munis'.

wordByWord.etymology: Dvandva: Skt. stuta ('praised') + mahita ('honoured, revered'); Pkt. thua + mahia.
wordByWord.notes: Closes the verse: the images (or this hymn) are 'praised and honoured' by Mahendra.

literalTranslation: Thus the eternal and the non-eternal images of the moon-like Lords of Jinas have been praised; by glorious Mahendra — a moon to the host of munis — (they are) praised and honoured.
idiomaticTranslation: And so they have all been hymned — both the everlasting images and those of mortal making — the icons of the moon-bright Lords of the Jinas. By glorious Mahendra, himself a moon for the assembly of sages, are they praised and honoured.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This is the sealing verse, and almost every word looks backward over the whole garland. The opening 'evaṁ' ('thus') gathers up the preceding 110 verses in a single gesture: everything just recited is now declared complete. The doubled 'sāsaya sāsaya' is best read as 'sāsaya a-sāsaya' — eternal and non-eternal — so that the worshipper, in one breath, embraces both the beginningless cosmic icons of the heavens and world-mountains and the historically founded shrines named through the hymn; nothing is left unbowed. The verb 'thuṇiyā' confirms that the act just performed was stuti, hymned praise, not mere enumeration. The most artful touch is the moon-motif: line 1 calls the supreme Jinas 'jiṇiṁda-caṁda', moon-lords, and line 2 turns the same image onto the poet himself — Mahendra is a 'caṁda', a moon, but for the 'muṇi-vaṁda', the company of sages. Thus the colophon humbly mirrors the praised in the praiser without claiming equality. The closing dvandva 'thua-mahiā' ('praised and honoured') rounds the verse on the same note it began: the whole 108-fold pilgrimage of words ends as it lived — as an offering of reverence. The verse is short by design; a colophon needs only to name its maker and set its seal.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the Jain warrant for image-veneration (pratimā-pūjā): a Jina-image is a worthy support for bhāva (devotional intention), and praising it cultivates merit and śubha-adhyavasāya. By yoking 'sāsaya' to 'a-sāsaya', the poet affirms that both the eternal, uncreated icons of the cosmos and the impermanent, human-consecrated images deserve the same homage — a quiet expression of the larger Jain reading of reality as both enduring in substance and ever-changing in mode. The act named here is stuti, the same devotional mood as the Caturviṁśati-stava: praise as a means of shedding karma and turning the mind toward liberation.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this verse, the worshipper consciously 'closes the garland' — mentally gathering every tīrtha and image just visited and offering the merit of the whole word-pilgrimage. It is a moment to register that the recitation was an act of bhakti, not a list, and to bow once more, inwardly, to all Jina-images everywhere, seen and unseen.

crossReferences.text: Logassa (Caturviṁśati-stava) — praise of the 24 Tīrthaṅkaras as the model act of stuti
crossReferences.source: Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

crossReferences.text: Aṣṭottarī Tīrthamālā vv.105–110 — the universalizing homage to all images and tīrthas of the three worlds that this colophon seals

sources.title: Jain Quantum (jainqq.org) — Jain literature search engine
sources.consultedFor: Cross-checking the Aṣṭottarī Tīrthamālā tradition, Mahendrasūri authorship, and śāśvata/aśāśvata image categories

notes: Line 1 prints 'સાસય સાસય' (sāsaya sāsaya). It is read here as the standard pair 'sāsaya a-sāsaya' (eternal / non-eternal), the second member's negative 'a-' lost in sandhi/recitation — consistent with this hymn's repeated śāśvata–aśāśvata image theme; a less likely alternative is emphatic reduplication ('eternal, truly eternal'). The book's reading is preserved verbatim and not normalized. Line 2 is a laudatory colophon naming the author Mahendra; the chain 'mahiṁda bhavahiṁda caṁda muṇivaṁda' is a string of honorific epithets, but the precise resolution of 'bhavahiṁda' (bhava-indra 'lord amid existence' vs. bhavya-indra 'chief of the worthy') and whether 'muṇivaṁda' is muni-vṛnda ('host of munis') or muni-vandya ('revered by munis') is uncertain; both renderings are devotionally compatible and neither alters the verse's sense. No verified critical edition with a word-gloss of this exact gāthā was located online, so glosses rest on standard Prakrit/Sanskrit lexicography.
